titleStmt.title: The Chargé in Korea (Lightner) to the Department of State

head.hi: The Chargé in Korea (Lightner) to the Department of State

div.p:
303. Rptd info CINCUNC, KCOMZ, Admiral Hanlon
Rear Admiral Byron H. Hanlon, USN, was appointed CINCUNC representative on the Combined Economic Board in Korea in August 1952. DIP 131 by other means. During the Ambassador’s farewell call Muccio left Korea on Sept. 8. on Pres Rhee Sept 7 Pres revealed his present thinking on several matters. While his views are generally not new they illustrate how his mind is presently working.

Rhee mentioned his appreciation for optimistic tone Ambassador’s remarks on accepting degree from Seoul University day before, particularly with respect to econ situation. Rhee said he felt Amb was probably right but then said he continued to be perturbed slow progress rehabilitation and continuing rise in cost of living. He referred to previous changes in official dollar-won exchange rate, each of which had been based on theory establishing more realistic rate. Instead of curbing inflation only noticeable effect had been to increase black market exchange rate proportionately. Now “people” were again talking about more realistic rate and Pres wondered why they cld not learn from experience of the past that net effect wld only be to increase black market rate to higher levels. Cost of living wld go up still higher and no one wld benefit.

First thing that had to be done, according to Rhee, was provide ROK with sound currency in which everyone wld have full confidence. This wld be easy if Amb cld convince Dept that ROK shld have loan of $300,000,000—mere pittance to US. Loan wld be converted into interest-bearing bonds that wld remain on deposit in US with sole function of providing backing to new ROK currency to be called “whan”. Old won wld be withdrawn from circulation and exchanged for whan at rate to be agreed upon.

(It will be recalled that Rhee has been advocating some such plan for many months and that all arguments to convince him that inflationary situation shld be stabilized before undertaking a currency conversion have fallen on deaf ears. On other hand, the FinMin appears to have more orthodox view on this.)

In reply to the Ambassador’s comments on the importance of sending a strong ROK delegation to the forthcoming UNGA Rhee mentioned he had great confidence in Col Limb and Amb Yang,Colonel B. C. Limb and Ambassador Y. C. Yang were the ROK observers at the United Nations. South Korea was a nonmember state. who he felt wld well represent his views, but that he did plan to send possibly one other person from Pusan to assist them. He had not yet decided whom he wld send. He then referred to fighting spirit of ROKA and need to
build up forces still more. He spoke of determination of the troops as well as of all ROK citizens to press the fight until Korea liberated; maybe not to Yalu but at least to the waist of peninsula; suggested ROKA spearhead attack, moving UN Forces to rear as reserves. He felt drive wld be successful and wld not provoke world war that UN feared.

When conversation turned to polit situation Amb hoped it wld remain stabilized so that full attn cld be focused on solving many problems needing attn. Rhee responded that unfortunately he was surrounded by traitors, many of whom were in pay of either Communists or Japs; that elements of the old Heungsa-danHeungsa-dan translates “Young Korea Academy” which was a cultural group bound to An Chang-ho, a democratic independence leader and rival to Rhee. The group joined the Korean Democratic Party in opposition to Rhee in 1952 (Henderson, Korea, The Politics of the Vortex, p. 300). with connections in North Korea were still influential and that many of the wealthy businessmen were working for selfish interests and in league with traitors. These groups were still powerful in the Natl Assembly and Rhee cld not take chance of permitting them to become stronger and perhaps eventually take over the Govt. He was therefore going ahead with his plan to introduce a new series of constitutional amendments which wld safeguard peoples rights and country’s security. He knew it wld be difficult put them through Assembly but he felt need was so urgent he wld have to take necessary steps get them adopted.

(Rhee gave no inkling as to his timing on forcing this issue. Newspapers full of speculation on this but have not linked question with effect, it wld have on discussion of Korean prog in UNGA. Rhee surely must realize it wld not help Korean case if it were discussed during period of polit turmoil in Korea. The foregoing consideration wld not necessarily delay submission of amendments to Natl Assembly but might postpone for a while resort to unconstitutional methods to force passage.)

Rhee covered much same ground and additional matters in long rambling conversation Sept 8 with Justice Douglas
William O. Douglas, Associate Justice of the U.S. Supreme Court. (at one point he expressed inability understand why US Govt had failed to act on his requests for a security pledge or pact. He said he realized that earlier ROK views favoring Pacific pact along lines of NATO were probably unrealistic since several Asiatic countries were not anti-Communist. Jap was notable example as it had not outlawed CP, was full of Commies, wanted trade with Iron Curtain countries and definitely was becoming more dangerous every day. The US had made a pact with its ex-enemy Jap but still refused to give similar assurance to its friend the ROK.). Douglas passed this on to me and I remarked that I understood American Govt was not opposed in principle to widening scope of its security arrangements in the Pacific but that a greater community of interest among participating powers was required before such a pack cld wisely
be contemplated. As far as bilateral pact was concerned I said I assumed principal objective of any security pact was to assure military support by one party to other party if either attacked; the US even without such pact had come to defense of ROK and was still here fighting. Under the circumstances with ROK now receiving full benefits of a security pact need for formalizing such pact hardly seemed urgent.
(Justice Douglas summing up his impression of Rhee afterwards remarked that anyway he must have been a fine revolutionary leader in his long struggle against the Jap.)
